text:
Avocado Egg Pita

One mixing bowl makes 4 lunches: mash, stuff, sauce, fridge. No stove, no reheating, and it travels well in a container or wrapped in foil.


This is the classic "assemble once, eat all week" combo meal: a creamy avocado-egg filling that keeps well on its own, paired with pita and sauce that you add fresh right before eating. Keeping the wet filling separate from the bread until serving time is the one trick that makes this hold up for days instead of going soggy by day two.

## Tips

- Store the filling and pita separately — combine only at serving time.
- Press plastic wrap directly onto the surface of the avocado mix before sealing the container; it slows browning noticeably.
- Swap the eggs for canned chickpeas (mashed) for a vegan version, or add a scoop of cottage cheese for even more protein.
- Works just as well in a wrap, over toast, or spooned onto crackers if you're out of pita.
- Pair with the [Everyday Yogurt-Tahini Sauce](/meal-prep/everyday-yogurt-tahini-sauce) for a tangy finish, or use hot sauce, tzatziki, or hummus instead.

Brown Rice

Cooks in one big batch, reheats without losing texture, and forms the base of dozens of different bowls throughout the week.


A big batch of brown rice is the backbone of easy weeknight meals — it's the neutral base that lets your proteins and vegetables rotate without repeating the same meal twice.

## Tips

- Cook a full rice-cooker batch on Sunday and portion into containers immediately.
- Spread rice on a sheet pan to cool quickly before refrigerating — this prevents sogginess.
- Freezes exceptionally well in flat, single-portion bags that thaw in minutes.
- Add a squeeze of lime or a drizzle of sesame oil after reheating to freshen the flavor.

Chicken & Rice Power Bowl

Combines three things you've likely already batch-cooked — chicken, rice, roasted vegetables — into one complete, portioned meal instead of three separate containers.


This is the "power bowl" pattern: a grain, a protein, and a vegetable, built once and portioned into ready-to-go meals for the week. It's built directly on top of the [Chicken Breast](/meal-prep/chicken-breast), [Brown Rice](/meal-prep/brown-rice), and [Roasted Vegetables](/meal-prep/roasted-vegetables) guides — if you're already batch-cooking those three, assembling this bowl adds almost no extra work.

## Tips

- Keep the sauce separate until serving — dressed rice gets soggy by day three.
- For a lower-calorie, cutting-focused version, swap half the rice for extra vegetables and keep the chicken portion the same — same protein, fewer carbs.
- Reheat the chicken and rice together, but add any raw or delicate toppings (herbs, avocado, a fried egg) fresh.
- Rotate the vegetable and sauce each week to keep the same base from feeling repetitive.

Chicken Breast

Cooks in bulk uniformly, holds moisture well when stored in its own juices, and is versatile across cuisines.


Chicken breast is one of the most reliable meal-prep proteins: it's cheap, cooks fast in bulk (sheet pan, grill, or instant pot), and takes on almost any marinade or spice blend.

## Tips

- Slice or shred right after cooking while it's still warm — it's easier to portion.
- Store in an airtight container with a spoonful of cooking liquid or sauce to keep it from drying out in the fridge.
- Freeze in individual portions so you can thaw only what you need.
- Pair with a grain and a roasted vegetable for a complete meal-prep bowl.

Everyday Yogurt-Tahini Sauce

Five minutes and five ingredients turns any bowl, wrap, or pita into something that tastes finished — and it keeps for a full week.


One sauce, made once, that upgrades a whole week of meal-prep bowls, wraps, and combo meals — this is what turns a plain container of protein and rice into something you actually look forward to eating. The Greek yogurt cuts the richness of the tahini and adds protein without watering down the flavor.

## Tips

- Make it thinner for a salad dressing, thicker for a wrap spread — the water amount is the only variable.
- Doesn't freeze well (the tahini and yogurt separate), but a week of fridge life is usually enough to use it up.
- Great on the [Avocado Egg Pita](/meal-prep/avocado-egg-pita), the [Chicken & Rice Power Bowl](/meal-prep/chicken-rice-power-bowl), or plain roasted vegetables.
- Swap lemon for lime and add a pinch of cumin for a different flavor direction without changing the base ratio.

Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait

A high-protein, low-effort breakfast that layers in under five minutes and holds up for four days — useful for a cutting phase where hitting a protein target without a lot of calories matters.


Layered parfaits solve the usual meal-prep breakfast problem — cereal goes soggy, yogurt alone gets boring — by keeping the crunchy element separate until the moment you eat. It's also one of the highest protein-per-calorie combo meals here, which makes it a good anchor for a cutting-focused week.

## Tips

- Nonfat plain Greek yogurt has roughly double the protein of flavored yogurt for the same calories — sweeten it yourself instead.
- Frozen berries work fine and are cheaper; just thaw briefly or eat them semi-frozen for texture.
- If you're not tracking calories closely, swap the honey for a mashed banana layer instead.
- Doesn't freeze — Greek yogurt's texture breaks down and turns grainy after thawing.

Overnight Oats

Zero cooking required, five minutes of assembly makes five breakfasts, and the base recipe is endlessly customizable.


Overnight oats are the lowest-effort meal-prep item on this list — just oats, liquid, and a fridge.

## Tips

- Use a 1:1 ratio of rolled oats to milk (dairy or plant-based), plus a spoonful of yogurt or chia seeds for extra creaminess.
- Prep 5 jars at once on Sunday — same base, different toppings each day (berries, banana, nut butter, cinnamon).
- Add mix-ins like nuts or granola right before eating so they stay crunchy instead of going soft overnight.
- Doesn't freeze well due to texture changes — stick to the 5-day fridge window.

Roasted Vegetables

One sheet pan can hold enough vegetables for the whole week, and roasting concentrates flavor so they still taste good reheated.


Roasted vegetables — broccoli, carrots, bell peppers, zucchini, or whatever's in season — are the easiest way to add fiber and color to every meal-prep container.

## Tips

- Cut vegetables to a similar size so they roast evenly.
- Toss with oil, salt, and pepper only — save fresh herbs or sauces for after reheating so they stay vibrant.
- Keep firmer vegetables (carrots, broccoli) separate from softer ones (zucchini, tomatoes) since they hold up differently over the week.
- Great cold in a grain bowl or reheated as a side.

Tuna Lettuce Wraps

A high-protein, low-calorie lunch with no cooking and no carbs beyond the vegetables — one of the leanest options here, built for a cutting phase or anyone limiting bread and rice.


Skipping the bread keeps this both fast to prep and light on calories relative to its protein content, which is exactly the trade-off that matters most on a cutting week. Keeping the lettuce separate from the filling until you eat is the only thing standing between this and a soggy, wilted wrap.

## Tips

- Canned salmon or canned chicken work as drop-in substitutes if you want variety through the week.
- The paper-towel trick for the lettuce matters more than it seems — wet leaves wilt within a day.
- Add a handful of avocado right before eating for healthy fats, rather than mixing it in ahead of time (it browns).
- Pack the tuna mix and lettuce in the same container with a divider if you don't want to carry two containers.

Balanced Week

A five-day rotation of five prep sessions on Sunday, built for someone who wants healthy, home-cooked meals on autopilot without eating the exact same thing every day.

Daily calorie target: 2000-2200 kcal. Protein focus: 90-110g/day.

Monday: breakfast - Overnight Oats; lunch - Chicken & Rice Power Bowl; dinner - Sheet-pan salmon with roasted vegetables and brown rice; snack - Apple with a tablespoon of peanut butter
Tuesday: breakfast - Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait; lunch - Avocado Egg Pita; dinner - Turkey chili with a side of brown rice; snack - A handful of almonds and a piece of fruit
Wednesday: breakfast - Overnight Oats; lunch - Tuna Lettuce Wraps; dinner - Chicken & Rice Power Bowl (leftovers); snack - Greek yogurt with a drizzle of honey
Thursday: breakfast - Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait; lunch - Avocado Egg Pita; dinner - Stir-fried beef or tofu with roasted vegetables over rice; snack - Carrot sticks with hummus
Friday: breakfast - Overnight Oats; lunch - Tuna Lettuce Wraps; dinner - Flex night — cook something fresh or order in; snack - Whatever's left in the fridge — a good night to clear it out


This plan assumes one real prep session on Sunday: batch-cook the rice, chicken, roasted vegetables, and oats, then assemble the [Avocado Egg Pita](/meal-prep/avocado-egg-pita), [Chicken & Rice Power Bowl](/meal-prep/chicken-rice-power-bowl), [Tuna Lettuce Wraps](/meal-prep/tuna-lettuce-wraps), and [Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait](/meal-prep/greek-yogurt-protein-parfait) on top of those components. The repetition across days is intentional — that's what makes meal prep sustainable — while five distinct combo meals keep the week from feeling monotonous.

## How to use this plan

- Shop Saturday, prep Sunday afternoon (2-3 hours covers all five components).
- Swap any dinner for whatever protein and vegetable is on sale that week — the rice and vegetable base doesn't care which protein sits on top.
- Friday is left open on purpose. Most people don't want to eat the same rotation five days straight, and it gives you a buffer if the week didn't go as planned.
- If 2000-2200 kcal runs high or low for you, adjust portion sizes rather than swapping meals — the ratios are what make this "balanced."

Cutting Week

A five-day, higher-protein, lower-calorie rotation for a fat-loss phase — built around the same combo meals as the Balanced Week, with carbs trimmed and protein pushed up.

Daily calorie target: 1500-1700 kcal. Protein focus: 120-140g/day.

Monday: breakfast - Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait; lunch - Tuna Lettuce Wraps; dinner - Grilled chicken breast with a large portion of roasted vegetables, no rice; snack - A hard-boiled egg and cucumber slices
Tuesday: breakfast - Egg whites scrambled with spinach; lunch - Chicken & Rice Power Bowl — half the rice, double the vegetables; dinner - Tuna Lettuce Wraps; snack - Celery with a small spoon of peanut butter
Wednesday: breakfast - Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait; lunch - Avocado Egg Pita filling over greens, no pita; dinner - Grilled chicken breast with a large portion of roasted vegetables, no rice; snack - A hard-boiled egg
Thursday: breakfast - Egg whites scrambled with spinach; lunch - Tuna Lettuce Wraps; dinner - Chicken & Rice Power Bowl — half the rice, double the vegetables; snack - Cucumber and cherry tomatoes
Friday: breakfast - Greek Yogurt Protein Parfait; lunch - Avocado Egg Pita filling over greens, no pita; dinner - Flex night — keep it protein-forward, watch the portion; snack - Whatever's left in the fridge


Same underlying prep as the [Balanced Week](/meal-prep/plans/balanced-week) — same chicken, same tuna, same yogurt — but the carb-heavy sides (rice, pita bread) are cut back or dropped so protein makes up a bigger share of every meal. That's the actual mechanism behind a "cutting" plan: not starvation, just shifting the ratio so you stay fuller on fewer calories.

## How to use this plan

- Cook the chicken and vegetables plain (just salt, pepper, oil) so you can portion by weight if you're tracking macros precisely.
- The [Everyday Yogurt-Tahini Sauce](/meal-prep/everyday-yogurt-tahini-sauce) is calorie-dense in small amounts — use it sparingly (a drizzle, not a ladle) to stay in range.
- 1500-1700 kcal is a starting point, not a rule — adjust portions to your own size, activity level, and how the week feels rather than following the number rigidly.
- If a snack or dinner leaves you genuinely hungry, add a larger portion of the vegetables first before adding more of anything calorie-dense — they're the cheapest way to add volume.

What makes a meal high-protein

A meal is generally considered high-protein when it provides at least 20-25 grams of protein per serving. Good sources include chicken breast, fish, eggs, Greek yogurt, tofu, lentils, and beans. High-protein meals help with satiety and muscle maintenance, which is especially useful for meal prep since they tend to keep you full for longer between meals.

Sodium guidelines for a healthy meal

Most health guidelines recommend keeping daily sodium intake under about 2,300mg, which works out to roughly 500-700mg per meal if eating three meals a day. Processed and packaged foods are the biggest source of excess sodium — a single serving of a heavily processed meal can easily exceed 1,000mg. Cooking from whole ingredients at home is one of the most reliable ways to control sodium intake.

metadata.title: Sodium guidelines for a healthy meal

text:
What Nutri-Score means

Nutri-Score is a front-of-pack nutrition label that grades food from A (healthiest) to E (least healthy) based on a formula that weighs negative factors (calories, sugar, saturated fat, sodium) against positive factors (fiber, protein, fruit/vegetable content). A grade of A or B generally indicates a more balanced nutritional profile, while D or E often signals a highly processed or energy-dense food that should be eaten in moderation rather than as a staple.

Why fiber matters in a balanced meal

Dietary fiber, found in vegetables, whole grains, legumes, and fruit, supports digestion, helps regulate blood sugar, and contributes to feeling full after eating. Most adults benefit from getting at least 25-30 grams of fiber per day, but the average diet falls well short of that. Pairing a starchy base like rice or pasta with a fiber-rich vegetable is a simple way to boost the fiber content of any meal.

metadata.title: Why fiber matters in a balanced meal

text:
Understanding NOVA processing groups

The NOVA classification groups foods into four categories by how processed they are: 1) unprocessed or minimally processed foods like fresh vegetables and plain meat, 2) processed culinary ingredients like oils and salt, 3) processed foods like canned vegetables or fresh bread, and 4) ultra-processed foods like packaged snacks, sugary drinks, and instant meals, which typically contain additives and ingredients not found in a home kitchen. Diets higher in NOVA group 4 foods are associated with worse health outcomes, so favoring groups 1-3 when possible is generally recommended.

The basics of batch cooking for meal prep

Batch cooking works best when you build meals from three components: a protein, a grain or starch, and a vegetable, each cooked in bulk separately and combined at serving time. This mix-and-match approach avoids the boredom of eating the exact same meal all week while still saving time. Cooling food quickly before refrigerating, using airtight containers, and portioning into single-serving sizes are the habits that make batch-cooked food actually taste good several days later.

metadata.title: The basics of batch cooking for meal prep
